title: Eduversal Competency Framework Content Backfill v1.0
description: Backfills 35 missing (compId, level) entries in the teaching-competency-framework.json (16 missing) and leadership-competency-framework.json (19 missing) source JSONs. The cross-module audit on 2026-05-08 found 16 TH + 19 AH cells empty in the source JSONs (not a Firestore seed gap — the source data was authored only at advanced/lead levels for these competencies). This backfill completes the matrix to 96/96 + 96/96 coverage by hand-authoring the missing awareness/practitioner cells in format-fidelity to the existing 161 hand-authored entries.
format_fidelity_notes: Each entry follows the exact shape of existing entries: reading (~1000-1300 chars, opens with 'At the Awareness/Practitioner level, teachers/school leaders are beginning/...' and includes Indonesia-context + Cambridge standard reference + Permendiknas reference where relevant), keyTakeaways (3 items), selfAssessment (5 statements), activity (task/output/duration/evidence). Level voice progression: awareness = 'beginning to', practitioner = 'consistently apply', advanced = 'systematically integrate', lead = 'lead and coach others'.

smc-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand vertical curriculum alignment — the way Cambridge subject content progresses from Lower Secondary Checkpoint through IGCSE to AS / A Level. A teacher at this stage can recognise that the same concept (e.g. cellular respiration, algebraic factorisation, persuasive writing) appears at each Cambridge stage at increasing cognitive demand. For example, a Grade 8 Science teacher in Surabaya reads the Cambridge Lower Secondary Checkpoint Science syllabus alongside the IGCSE Coordinated Sciences (0654) syllabus and notices that 'separation of mixtures' is taught at descriptive level in Year 8 but progresses to quantitative analysis in Year 10. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 1, which requires deep subject knowledge across the progression Cambridge offers, and with Permendiknas No. 16/2007, Article 3, on subject-content competency. At this stage, the teacher is not yet adjusting their own scheme of work in response to vertical-alignment insights, but is building awareness that what they teach today is preparation for what students will face two and four years later.
keyTakeaways: Read your subject's Cambridge syllabus document for the year ABOVE and the year BELOW your current teaching assignment.
Identify three concepts that appear at multiple Cambridge stages with increasing cognitive demand.
Notice where prerequisite skills are assumed at the next stage that you are responsible for building this year.
selfAssessment: I can name the Cambridge stages above and below my current teaching year (e.g., Checkpoint → IGCSE → AS).
I can locate the syllabus documents for adjacent stages without help.
I can identify at least three concepts that appear in spiral progression across stages.
I can describe how cognitive demand increases for the same topic at the next stage.
I can explain why a Grade 9 student needs strong foundation in topic X for their Grade 11 Cambridge paper.

activity.task: Choose three core concepts in your subject. For each, locate where it appears in the Cambridge syllabus at the stage below, your current stage, and the stage above. Create a vertical progression table showing how the assessment objective and cognitive demand evolve.
activity.output: A three-row vertical alignment table per concept, showing the syllabus reference + AO code + cognitive demand at each stage.
activity.evidence: Upload the completed table to your IPDL with a brief reflection on which transitions surprised you.

smc-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand that curriculum review is not a one-time activity but an ongoing professional practice. A teacher at this stage can recognise that schemes of work, lesson resources, and assessment tasks should be reviewed regularly using student performance data and Cambridge examiner reports. For example, an English Language teacher in Jakarta reads the most recent Cambridge IGCSE First Language English (0500) examiner report after the May/June series and notices that students network-wide struggled with persuasive devices identification — and reflects that her own Year 10 students likely face the same gap. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.1, which expects teachers to engage in continuous professional learning, and with the Eduversal Network Standard requiring schemes of work to be updated within 4 weeks of Cambridge results release. At this stage, the teacher is not yet contributing to formal department-level curriculum review, but is building the habit of reading examiner reports + reflecting on personal practice + noting candidate adjustments to next year's planning.
keyTakeaways: Read the Cambridge examiner report for your subject after each results release window.
Identify three patterns in network candidate performance that may apply to your own students.
Begin a 'next year' notes file where you log specific changes you want to make to your scheme of work.
selfAssessment: I know where to find Cambridge examiner reports for my subject.
I have read at least one examiner report this academic year.
I can identify at least three improvement areas from a single examiner report.
I keep notes on changes I want to make to my next-year scheme of work.
I have raised at least one curriculum review observation in a department PLC.

activity.task: Read the most recent examiner report for your Cambridge subject. Highlight three patterns where candidates network-wide underperformed. For each, describe one specific change you would make to your own scheme of work.
activity.output: An annotated examiner report + a one-page note describing your three planned scheme-of-work changes for next year.
activity.evidence: Upload the annotated report and your notes to your IPDL.

smc-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, teachers consistently engage in curriculum review as a regular practice — not an event triggered only by results release. A teacher at this stage uses multiple data sources (Cambridge results, EASE results, classroom assessment data, examiner reports) to evaluate the effectiveness of their scheme of work and makes targeted adjustments. For example, a Mathematics teacher in Bandung notices through EASE I results that her Year 9 students underperformed on geometric reasoning compared to their network peers, cross-checks against the IGCSE Mathematics (0580) examiner report from the prior year, and decides to redesign three lessons in her geometry sequence to include more proof-based reasoning earlier. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.1 (continuous professional learning) and with Eduversal F4 outcome data feedback into instructional practice. At this stage, the teacher contributes review insights to department PLC discussions and proposes specific scheme-of-work revisions, but is not yet leading whole-department curriculum review cycles.
keyTakeaways: Triangulate your students' performance against EASE network data + Cambridge examiner reports + your own classroom assessments.
Document specific scheme-of-work changes with the data evidence that justifies them.
Bring at least one curriculum review insight per term to your department PLC.
selfAssessment: I review my scheme of work at least termly using student performance evidence.
I can cite specific data sources that drove my last three scheme-of-work changes.
I document my planned changes with evidence trails, not just intuition.
I bring curriculum review insights to PLC discussions regularly.
I can describe one scheme-of-work change I made this year and the student-outcome evidence that justified it.

activity.task: Conduct a mid-year scheme-of-work review for one subject you teach. Triangulate three data sources (EASE results, classroom assessment data, Cambridge examiner report). Identify three specific changes you will make for the second semester, each backed by data evidence.
activity.output: A completed scheme-of-work review document with three data-justified change proposals.
activity.evidence: Upload the review document to your IPDL and present it briefly in your next department PLC.

lcp-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand higher-order questioning and Bloom's Taxonomy as essential tools for moving beyond recall-level instruction. A teacher at this stage can identify the difference between recall questions (What is photosynthesis?) and analytical questions (Why does photosynthesis rate decrease at very high light intensities?). For example, a Grade 11 Biology teacher in Yogyakarta reviews her last week's questioning patterns from her IGCSE class and notices that 80% of her questions were knowledge-recall — well below the analytical demand of the Cambridge IGCSE Biology Paper 2 mark scheme. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.2, which requires teachers to use a range of teaching strategies that engage learners and enable progress, and with Permendikdasmen No. 13/2025 on the deep-learning approach (pendekatan pembelajaran mendalam). At this stage, the teacher is not yet systematically planning higher-order questions in advance, but is becoming aware of the cognitive demand gap between her current questioning and what Cambridge papers actually require.
keyTakeaways: Audit your last lesson's questions: how many were recall-level vs analysis-level?
Learn the six Bloom's Taxonomy levels and recognise example verbs for each.
Begin planning at least two analysis-level questions explicitly in your lesson plan, not improvising them in the moment.
selfAssessment: I can name the six levels of Bloom's Taxonomy and give an example question at each level.
I can audit a lesson and classify questions by cognitive demand.
I plan at least one HOTs question explicitly in my lesson plan.
I notice the cognitive level my Cambridge paper requires of students.
I give students at least 5 seconds of wait time after asking an analysis-level question.

activity.task: Record (audio or written) the questions you ask in one full lesson. Classify each by Bloom's level. Compute the proportion of recall vs analysis-or-higher questions. Plan three explicit HOTs questions for your next lesson and note the wait time you used for each.
activity.output: An annotated question log + a follow-up lesson plan with three HOTs questions explicitly named.
activity.evidence: Upload the question log + follow-up plan to your IPDL with a brief reflection on your typical questioning pattern.

lcp-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand metacognition — students' awareness and management of their own learning — as a teachable skill, not an innate trait. A teacher at this stage can recognise the difference between a student who thinks 'I do not know this' (a fixed observation) and a student who thinks 'I do not yet understand X because I haven't applied Y' (a metacognitive observation that points to action). For example, a Year 7 Mathematics teacher in Bali notices that her students who struggle most are also the ones who do not articulate which step of a problem confuses them — they simply give up. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.2 on engaging learners in their own learning, and with Permendikdasmen No. 13/2025 emphasising deep-learning where students take ownership of their thinking. At this stage, the teacher is not yet teaching specific metacognitive routines, but is becoming aware that her stronger students think about thinking and her weaker students do not — and this gap is teachable.
keyTakeaways: Metacognition is a learnable skill, not an inherent trait — strong students can be taught it.
Listen to how students explain their thinking: vague vs specific reveals metacognitive maturity.
Begin asking 'How did you decide that?' as often as 'What is the answer?' in your classroom.
selfAssessment: I can explain the difference between cognition and metacognition.
I can recognise students with strong vs weak metacognitive habits in my classroom.
I ask 'how did you think about that?' regularly, not just 'what is the answer?'
I model my own thinking aloud when working through problems.
I have introduced at least one explicit metacognitive routine (e.g., a planning checklist) to my students.

activity.task: For one week, end every lesson with a 90-second 'metacognitive exit ticket': students answer 'What is one thing I am still unsure about, and what specifically would help me understand it?'. Read the responses and note three patterns about how your students think about their own learning.
activity.output: A summary of the week's exit ticket responses + three patterns you noticed.
activity.evidence: Upload the response summary and your patterns observation to your IPDL.

afl-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand data-informed teaching — using student performance data to identify learning gaps and design targeted intervention. A teacher at this stage can recognise that EASE results, classroom assessments, and exit ticket data are not just records of past performance but signals for next-week instructional decisions. For example, a Mathematics teacher in Semarang reviews her Grade 8 students' EASE I results and notices that 70% of her class scored below the network median on algebraic manipulation — a signal that her algebra unit needs targeted intervention before moving to functions. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 5.2, which expects teachers to use assessment data effectively to inform teaching, and with the Eduversal Domain 5 (Assessment & Feedback) data-informed instructional pivot. At this stage, the teacher is not yet running structured intervention cycles, but is beginning to read assessment data as forward-looking diagnosis rather than backward-looking judgement.
keyTakeaways: Assessment data is forward-looking diagnosis, not backward-looking judgement — it tells you what to teach next.
Disaggregate class results: who specifically struggled with which question? Patterns drive intervention.
Begin one targeted intervention based on data — even a 10-minute mini-lesson for the bottom-third matters.
selfAssessment: I review my class's assessment results within one week of administration.
I can identify which students underperformed and on which specific objectives.
I plan at least one targeted instructional response based on assessment data each unit.
I disaggregate class data by student or sub-group, not just look at averages.
I have told my students how their data informs my next-lesson decisions.

activity.task: Take your most recent class assessment. Disaggregate the results by student and by question/objective. Identify the three students or sub-groups who most need targeted intervention, and the one specific concept each one needs. Plan a 10-minute targeted mini-lesson for the next week.
activity.output: A disaggregated data summary + a targeted mini-lesson plan for the bottom-third or struggling sub-group.
activity.evidence: Upload the data summary and mini-lesson plan to your IPDL with a follow-up note after delivery.

afl-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand student self-assessment and peer-assessment as essential skills students must learn — not as time-savers for the teacher. A teacher at this stage can recognise that students who can accurately self-assess their work against clear success criteria become better learners; students who cannot self-assess remain dependent on the teacher for every judgement. For example, a Year 9 English teacher in Surabaya gives her class a peer-assessment rubric for an opinion paragraph and notices that students give vague feedback ('good' / 'add more') rather than specific feedback because they have not been taught how to use the rubric. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 5.1, which expects teachers to develop students' capacity to evaluate their own learning, and with Permendikdasmen No. 13/2025 emphasising deep-learning where students take ownership. At this stage, the teacher is not yet running structured self/peer-assessment routines, but is becoming aware that this is a teachable skill, not an automatic capability students arrive with.
keyTakeaways: Self-assessment and peer-assessment are skills that must be taught — they don't happen by giving students a rubric.
Model self-assessment by talking through your own thinking against the success criteria.
Begin with structured peer-assessment using sentence stems before moving to free-form feedback.
selfAssessment: I provide students with success criteria they can use to self-assess.
I model self-assessment for students by working through an example aloud.
I have used at least one structured peer-assessment routine this term.
I notice when students self-assess inaccurately and address it constructively.
I can describe how students' self-assessment habits have changed across the year.

activity.task: Choose a piece of student work you will assess next week. Develop a clear, student-friendly success criteria. Have students self-assess BEFORE you mark, then compare. Identify three patterns: where do students under-rate themselves? Over-rate? And what gap does that reveal about their understanding of the criteria?
activity.output: Annotated student work showing the comparison + a three-pattern observation log.
activity.evidence: Upload the annotated work and pattern log to your IPDL.

afl-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, teachers consistently use student self-assessment and peer-assessment as embedded routines — not occasional activities. A teacher at this stage has trained students to use success criteria accurately and uses self/peer-assessment data to identify learning gaps that teacher-only marking might miss. For example, a Mathematics teacher in Tangerang routinely has students self-assess against a four-criteria rubric before submitting work, then collects self-assessments alongside the work itself. She notices over time that students who consistently over-rate their own answers tend to struggle on summative assessments — a signal she can use for early intervention. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 5.1 on developing student self-evaluation capacity. At this stage, the teacher uses self/peer-assessment as a regular instructional tool but does not yet share peer-assessment leadership routines across the department.
keyTakeaways: Treat student self-assessment data as instructional intelligence — students who over-rate are an early-intervention signal.
Use peer-assessment to free yourself from being the only judge — but only after students have been trained on the criteria.
Compare self-assessment against teacher-assessment and surface the gap as a learning conversation.
selfAssessment: Self-assessment routines are embedded in at least 50% of my marked assignments.
I have trained students to use rubrics so their self-assessments roughly match my marking.
I use self-assessment data to identify students who need early intervention.
I run structured peer-assessment activities at least twice per term.
I can describe how my use of self/peer-assessment has changed my class's learning culture.

activity.task: For one full unit, have students self-assess every formative task. Maintain a parallel record of teacher-assessment vs self-assessment. At the end of the unit, identify the three students whose self-assessment most diverged from teacher-assessment. Have a structured conversation with each about what they noticed.
activity.output: A unit-level self-vs-teacher assessment log + reflection notes from three student conversations.
activity.evidence: Upload the log + reflection notes to your IPDL.

icp-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand Cambridge Global Perspectives as a curriculum component (and pedagogical approach) that builds critical thinking, research, and reflection skills across subjects. A teacher at this stage can recognise that Global Perspectives is not just a stand-alone subject in the IGCSE / Cambridge programmes — it is a way of structuring inquiry that any subject teacher can adopt. For example, a Geography teacher in Medan reads the Cambridge IGCSE Global Perspectives (0457) syllabus and notices the same skill set (analyse a global issue from multiple perspectives, evaluate sources, construct a reasoned argument) is exactly what she wants her own Geography students to do — even though she does not formally teach the Global Perspectives subject. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 on critical engagement with research and Permendikdasmen No. 13/2025 deep-learning. At this stage, the teacher is not yet redesigning her own units to embed Global Perspectives skills, but is becoming aware that the curriculum framework offers a shareable language for skills she values.
keyTakeaways: Cambridge Global Perspectives is a skill framework that can be adopted across any subject — not just the standalone subject.
Read the Global Perspectives syllabus for the four core skills it develops: research, analysis, evaluation, reflection.
Notice where your subject already develops these skills implicitly — they are teachable explicitly.
selfAssessment: I can name the four core skills the Cambridge Global Perspectives syllabus develops.
I can identify where these skills already appear in my own subject's scheme of work.
I have read at least one Cambridge Global Perspectives sample paper or examiner report.
I can describe one unit in my subject that could explicitly develop a Global Perspectives skill.
I have discussed Global Perspectives skill integration with at least one colleague.

activity.task: Read the Cambridge Global Perspectives syllabus and one sample assessment. Map the four core skills (research / analysis / evaluation / reflection) to specific units in your own subject's scheme of work. Identify one unit where you could explicitly develop one of these skills next term.
activity.output: A four-by-N skills mapping + a one-paragraph proposal for an explicit Global Perspectives skill integration in your subject.
activity.evidence: Upload the mapping and proposal to your IPDL.

icp-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand action research — small-scale, classroom-based investigation of a teaching question — as a professional practice they can engage with. A teacher at this stage can recognise that effective teaching is not a settled body of knowledge but a continuous experiment, and that they can structure their own classroom to test ideas. For example, an English teacher in Jakarta wonders whether her students would write better paragraphs if she required them to plan with PEEL structure first; she decides to teach two classes the same lesson, with PEEL-planning required in one and free-style in the other, and compare the resulting paragraphs. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 on critical engagement with research and 6.1 on continuous professional learning. At this stage, the teacher is not yet running formal action research cycles or sharing findings with colleagues, but is beginning to ask 'I wonder if...' questions about her own teaching that lead to small structured experiments.
keyTakeaways: Action research starts with 'I wonder if...' — a curious question about your own teaching.
Small experiments are valid: comparing two classes, two lessons, or two task variations is enough.
Document what you tried + what you observed, even informally — that becomes evidence-based practice.
selfAssessment: I have wondered, in writing, about a specific teaching question this academic year.
I can describe one small experiment I ran in my own classroom.
I noticed and recorded the difference between control and intervention conditions.
I can explain why action research is a professional, not amateur, activity.
I have shared one informal action-research finding with a colleague.

activity.task: Identify one specific 'I wonder if...' question about your own teaching. Design a simple comparison: teach two parallel classes the same lesson with one variable changed. Document what you observed. Reflect on what the comparison did and did not reveal.
activity.output: A one-page action research note: question, design, observations, what you would do differently next time.
activity.evidence: Upload the action research note to your IPDL.

icp-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, teachers run small-scale action research as a regular practice — not a one-off curiosity. A teacher at this stage formulates investigable questions, designs simple controlled comparisons, collects evidence (student work, exit tickets, engagement data), and uses findings to adjust her teaching. For example, a Science teacher in Surabaya investigates whether using analogies before introducing a new concept improves Year 9 retention; she runs the experiment across two parallel classes for three lessons, collects exit ticket data, and concludes that the analogy approach correlates with a 15-point improvement on follow-up assessment. She shares this with her department PLC and three colleagues adopt the routine. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 (research-engaged practice) and with Eduversal's Monday Morning Commitment loop where evidence-based instructional changes are made visible to the team. At this stage, the teacher contributes findings to the department but does not yet co-author network-level professional resources or lead Eduversal-wide research initiatives.
keyTakeaways: Run at least one structured classroom action research cycle per term.
Document the question, design, evidence, and conclusion in a way colleagues can review.
Share findings in your department PLC — even small wins build collective knowledge.
selfAssessment: I run at least one structured action research cycle per term.
I can describe my last cycle: question, design, evidence, conclusion.
I disaggregate evidence to surface unexpected patterns, not just confirm hypotheses.
I have shared at least one finding in a department PLC this academic year.
I can identify one teaching change I made because of action research, not because of intuition.

activity.task: Design and run a structured action research cycle this term. Formulate the question. Design a comparison. Collect evidence systematically. Analyse and document conclusions. Present the cycle in a department PLC.
activity.output: A structured action research write-up (1-2 pages) + PLC presentation slides or notes.
activity.evidence: Upload the write-up and presentation materials to your IPDL.

pie-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand mentoring and contribution to school development as part of their professional identity — not optional add-ons. A teacher at this stage can recognise that experienced teachers in their school have professional responsibility to support newer colleagues, and that whole-school improvement depends on individuals contributing beyond their own classroom. For example, a Mathematics teacher with five years' experience in Bandung is asked to mentor a new colleague joining mid-year; she initially feels that mentoring is 'extra work' on top of her teaching, but begins to see that supporting the new colleague's success is an investment in her own department's quality. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.2 on collaboration with colleagues and Permendiknas 13/2007 on competency dimensions including kewirausahaan (innovation/development). At this stage, the teacher is not yet leading whole-school initiatives, but is becoming aware that mid-career and senior teachers carry responsibilities beyond classroom delivery.
keyTakeaways: Mentoring early-career colleagues is a professional responsibility for experienced teachers, not an optional kindness.
School improvement depends on individuals contributing beyond their classroom — this is part of the role.
Begin by offering one specific support to a less-experienced colleague this term — co-planning a lesson, observing them, sharing a resource.
selfAssessment: I can explain why experienced teachers have responsibility for newer colleagues.
I have offered specific support to a less-experienced colleague this term.
I have contributed to at least one school-improvement initiative this academic year.
I see mentoring as professional development for me, not just for the mentee.
I can describe one school-level outcome that depends on individuals contributing beyond their classroom.

activity.task: Identify one less-experienced colleague in your school. Offer one specific, defined support this term: co-plan a lesson, observe them and offer 'I noticed / I wonder' feedback, or share three teaching resources with brief notes on when each works best.
activity.output: A brief reflection on what you offered and what you noticed about the colleague's response and your own learning.
activity.evidence: Upload the reflection to your IPDL.

pie-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, teachers actively mentor early-career colleagues and contribute to whole-school improvement initiatives as part of their professional rhythm. A teacher at this stage formally mentors at least one new colleague per academic year, contributes to at least one school-improvement workstream, and shares teaching resources or expertise across the department or school. For example, an experienced Indonesian Language teacher in Yogyakarta is paired with a first-year colleague through the Eduversal Mentor-Mentee Contract; they meet fortnightly, observe each other's lessons, and the experienced teacher contributes resources to the department's shared lesson library. She also serves on the school's bilingual instruction working group. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.2 on collaboration and the Eduversal Mentor-Base certification curriculum. At this stage, the teacher mentors and contributes regularly but does not yet lead whole-school improvement initiatives or co-author network resources.
keyTakeaways: Mentor at least one early-career colleague formally each academic year using the Mentor-Mentee Contract.
Contribute to one school-improvement workstream per year (e.g., bilingual support, assessment moderation, vertical alignment).
Treat mentoring and school contribution as evidence of your own professional development, not as obligations.
selfAssessment: I have formally mentored at least one early-career colleague this academic year.
I contribute to at least one school-improvement workstream regularly.
I share teaching resources actively with my department's shared library.
I can describe one whole-school outcome I helped achieve this year.
I have completed the Eduversal Mentor-Base certification (or equivalent).

activity.task: If you are not already mentoring, request a mentor pairing through your Subject Leader. If you already mentor, document the cycle this term: meeting frequency, focus topics, observations exchanged, resources shared. Reflect on what your mentee taught you.
activity.output: A termly mentor cycle log + a 200-word reflection on bidirectional learning.
activity.duration: 12 hours across the term (fortnightly meetings + observation pairs)
activity.evidence: Upload the cycle log and reflection to your IPDL.

cce-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand cross-departmental and cross-school collaboration as more than occasional meetings — as a structural feature of effective curriculum and shared practice. A teacher at this stage can recognise that the same skill (e.g., persuasive writing, data interpretation, structured argument) appears in multiple subjects and that students benefit when teachers across departments use shared language and shared expectations. For example, a History teacher in Bandung notices that her Year 10 students struggle with structured essay writing — and reflects that this same skill is also taught (with different vocabulary) in English Language and in Geography. She wonders whether her department could agree with English and Geography on a shared framework like PEEL paragraphs across all three subjects. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.2 on collaboration with colleagues and the Eduversal Domain 6 (Professional Practice) cross-curricular dimension. At this stage, the teacher is not yet leading cross-departmental initiatives, but is becoming aware that her students would benefit from cross-departmental coherence.
keyTakeaways: Skills taught in your subject are often taught in others too — students benefit from shared language across subjects.
Cross-departmental collaboration is structural curriculum coherence, not a meeting-extras add-on.
Begin by identifying one transferable skill your subject shares with at least one other subject.
selfAssessment: I can name three skills my subject teaches that are also taught in other subjects.
I have had at least one informal conversation with a colleague from another department about a shared skill.
I notice when students bring confusion about expectations across subjects.
I have read or contributed to at least one cross-departmental document this academic year.
I can describe how my school co-ordinates expectations across departments (or notice the absence of co-ordination).

activity.task: Identify one skill that appears in your subject and at least one other subject (e.g., structured argument, data interpretation, source evaluation). Talk with one colleague who teaches that other subject. Compare how each of you teaches the skill. Note what is consistent and what diverges.
activity.output: A two-paragraph note: skill identified, what is consistent across subjects, what diverges, and what consistency would help students.
activity.evidence: Upload the note to your IPDL.

cce-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, teachers are beginning to understand the wider Cambridge professional community — webinars, online forums, examiner training, regional Cambridge networks — as accessible resources that connect their classroom to a global teaching network. A teacher at this stage can recognise that Cambridge International offers numerous free professional resources (Teacher Support site, Cambridge Schools Online, examiner training, regional online events) and that engaging with these resources brings external expertise back into their teaching. For example, a Chemistry teacher in Solo discovers Cambridge's recorded examiner training videos online for IGCSE Chemistry (0620) and learns directly from chief examiners how Paper 2 questions are constructed and marked. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.1 on continuous professional learning and the Eduversal Network Standard valuing external benchmarking. At this stage, the teacher is not yet contributing to the Cambridge community (presenting, examining, peer-reviewing), but is becoming aware that the Cambridge professional community exists and is accessible.
keyTakeaways: Cambridge International offers free professional resources accessible from any partner school — register for the Teacher Support site.
Engaging with examiner training and online forums brings global teaching expertise back to your classroom.
Begin with one Cambridge professional resource per month — a webinar, an examiner report, or a forum post.
selfAssessment: I have a Cambridge Teacher Support site account and have logged in this academic year.
I have watched at least one Cambridge examiner training video for my subject.
I have read at least one Cambridge professional forum or community post.
I can name three free Cambridge professional resources accessible to me.
I have shared at least one external Cambridge resource with a colleague.

activity.task: Register for the Cambridge Teacher Support site if you have not already. Locate three resources for your subject: one examiner training video, one Cambridge published article or forum post, and one resource you have not seen before. Engage with one resource fully and share something useful with a colleague.
activity.output: A list of the three resources you found + a brief note on what you shared and the colleague's response.
activity.evidence: Upload the list and shared note to your IPDL.

cce-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, teachers regularly engage with the Cambridge professional community as a structured part of their CPD. A teacher at this stage attends Cambridge webinars, participates in regional Cambridge events, contributes to forums, and applies external expertise to their teaching. For example, a Mathematics teacher in Tangerang attends a Cambridge regional event in Singapore on teaching IGCSE Mathematics with manipulatives, returns and trials three of the techniques in her Year 9 class, then writes up her experience for the school newsletter and shares with her department. She also participates in an online Cambridge teacher forum where she has gained reputation for sharing thoughtful resources. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 6.1 on continuous professional learning + 6.2 on collaboration. At this stage, the teacher is engaged with the Cambridge community as a participant but not yet as a network leader (presenter, examiner, regional facilitator).
keyTakeaways: Attend at least one regional Cambridge event per academic year — even an online webinar counts.
When you bring back external expertise, document and share — not just for credit but for collective learning.
Build presence in one Cambridge professional forum: contribute thoughtfully and consistently.
selfAssessment: I attend at least one Cambridge regional event or sustained webinar series per year.
I have applied at least three Cambridge-network ideas to my own teaching this year.
I share Cambridge-derived resources with my department on a recurring basis.
I participate actively in at least one Cambridge online forum.
I can describe specific changes to my teaching that came from Cambridge community engagement.

activity.task: Identify one upcoming Cambridge regional event, webinar series, or online community of practice in your subject. Register and engage. After the event, document three insights, three classroom-trial intentions, and present to your department PLC.
activity.output: Event participation log + three-insight write-up + PLC presentation materials.
activity.evidence: Upload the materials to your IPDL.

evsi-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand Cambridge school evaluation — the formal Cambridge tools and frameworks for assessing whole-school readiness, quality, and centre status. A leader at this stage can recognise that Cambridge Centre evaluation is a structured process (not a one-time inspection) and that Cambridge provides specific self-evaluation tools schools can use proactively. For example, a newly appointed Academic Coordinator in Bandung discovers the Cambridge Schools Online self-evaluation framework and begins reading the 'Centre quality assurance' documentation, recognising that her school's last formal Cambridge inspection findings can be re-examined alongside this framework to identify gaps before the next inspection cycle. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 2023 area 9 (operations and management) and Permendiknas 63/2009 on quality assurance. At this stage, the leader is not yet running formal self-evaluation cycles, but is becoming aware that Cambridge offers structured tools for whole-school evaluation that go beyond reactive inspection response.
keyTakeaways: Cambridge offers structured self-evaluation tools — they are accessible to any partner school, not reserved for inspection time.
Read the most recent Cambridge inspection report for your school alongside the Cambridge self-evaluation framework.
Begin a 'gap awareness' file: where does our practice diverge from the Cambridge framework, even if not flagged in inspection?
selfAssessment: I can name the Cambridge framework documents that govern Centre quality.
I have read the most recent Cambridge inspection report for my school.
I can identify three areas where Cambridge expectations diverge from current school practice.
I know how to access Cambridge Schools Online and the Centre quality documentation.
I have discussed the Cambridge evaluation framework with at least one colleague this term.

activity.task: Locate the Cambridge Schools Online self-evaluation framework for your school's centre type. Compare against your school's most recent Cambridge inspection report. Identify three areas where school practice could be strengthened proactively (not waiting for the next inspection). Discuss with another leadership colleague.
activity.output: A three-area proactive strengthening note + colleague discussion record.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL with reflection on what surprised you.

evsi-3_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, school leaders consistently use Cambridge self-evaluation tools to drive school improvement, alongside Indonesian regulatory frameworks (Akreditasi BAN-S/M, Standar Nasional Pendidikan). A leader at this stage runs annual or termly self-evaluation cycles, documents findings, and uses them to inform the School Improvement Plan and RKAS allocation. For example, an Academic Coordinator in Surabaya runs the Cambridge self-evaluation alongside the Akreditasi self-evaluation each year, identifies overlapping priorities, and produces a single integrated school-improvement priority list rather than two parallel ones. The integration reduces duplicated work and surfaces priorities both bodies value. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 9.1-9.2 + Permendiknas 63/2009 (SPMI). At this stage, the leader runs cycles competently and uses findings strategically but does not yet co-design self-evaluation tools or contribute to network-level evaluation methodology.
keyTakeaways: Run formal self-evaluation cycles annually or termly — and integrate Cambridge + Indonesian frameworks rather than running them separately.
Use self-evaluation findings to drive RKAS and SIP, not just to file reports.
Document findings transparently so they enable next-year improvement and inform stakeholder communication.
selfAssessment: I run at least one formal Cambridge self-evaluation cycle each year.
I integrate Cambridge findings with Akreditasi and SNP findings rather than handling them separately.
Self-evaluation findings have shaped at least three RKAS allocation decisions this year.
I can describe how this year's SIP was informed by self-evaluation evidence.
I share self-evaluation findings transparently with the leadership team and yayasan.

activity.task: Conduct an integrated annual self-evaluation: Cambridge framework + Akreditasi self-evaluation simultaneously. Identify the three priorities most strongly supported by both frameworks. Map each to a specific RKAS or SIP intervention.
activity.output: Integrated self-evaluation report + three-priority intervention map.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL and discuss in the next ALT meeting.

evsi-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand that leading change is a structured discipline — not a force-of-personality event. A leader at this stage can recognise that successful school change requires more than announcing a new policy: it requires diagnosis, sequencing, communication, monitoring, and adjustment over time. For example, a newly promoted School Principal in Yogyakarta inherits a school where lesson planning quality varies widely; she initially intends to issue a new lesson-planning policy and expect compliance, but begins reading change leadership literature (Fullan, Heath brothers) and recognises that policy alone rarely produces sustained practice change. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 2023 area 1 (strategic leadership) and Permendikbud 6/2018 managerial duties. At this stage, the leader is not yet planning structured change initiatives, but is becoming aware that change leadership is a learnable competency, not a personal trait.
keyTakeaways: Successful school change requires diagnosis, sequencing, and monitoring — not just policy announcement.
Read at least one change-leadership text (Fullan, Heath, Kotter) — this is professional literature for school leaders.
Notice the difference between change announced and change adopted in your school's recent history.
selfAssessment: I can name at least three stages of a structured change-leadership cycle.
I have read at least one change-leadership text this year.
I can describe one change initiative in my school's history that succeeded and one that did not — and reflect on why.
I notice the gap between policy announced and practice changed.
I can explain why change-leadership skill is a competency, not a personality trait.

activity.task: Choose one school-change initiative from the past three years (succeeded or failed). Map it against a structured change-leadership framework (e.g., Kotter's 8 steps or Fullan's three pillars). Identify which steps were strong and which were weak.
activity.output: A change-initiative case study + a one-page reflection on what you would do differently.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

evsi-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, school leaders consistently lead structured change initiatives — diagnose, plan, sequence, monitor, adjust — rather than relying on policy announcement alone. A leader at this stage uses an explicit change-leadership framework, communicates the rationale clearly, builds coalitions, and maintains momentum across the often 18-24 month arc that practice change requires. For example, a Principal in Tangerang implements a new walkthrough cadence in her school: she begins with diagnostic walkthroughs herself, recruits two teacher-leader champions, runs three months of paired walkthrough training before extending to the wider team, monitors implementation, and adjusts the cadence based on what she observes. The initiative reaches sustained practice 14 months after launch. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 1 (strategic) + 6 (capacity building). At this stage, the leader leads change competently within her school but does not yet lead network-level change or coach other principals on change leadership.
keyTakeaways: Successful change typically takes 18-24 months — plan accordingly and commit to the duration.
Build a coalition of champions early — change rarely succeeds when imposed from one position.
Monitor implementation actively and adjust — initial design rarely survives contact with reality intact.
selfAssessment: I lead at least one structured change initiative per year using an explicit framework.
I plan change with diagnosis, sequencing, and monitoring — not just announcement.
I build a coalition of champions, not rely on positional authority alone.
I monitor implementation actively and adjust based on observation.
I can describe a change I led to sustained practice across 18+ months.

activity.task: Document a change initiative you are currently leading in your school. Map it against an explicit change-leadership framework. Identify three risks to sustained adoption and your specific monitoring + adjustment plan.
activity.output: A change-initiative leadership document with framework mapping, risk analysis, and monitoring plan.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL and discuss in your next coaching session with HQ Director.

cial-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand structured lesson observation and instructional feedback as core leadership practices — not annual appraisal events. A leader at this stage can recognise that walkthrough observation (5-10 minute, formative, frequent) is fundamentally different from formal supervision (full-lesson, summative, occasional), and that both are needed. For example, a newly promoted Cambridge Coordinator in Jakarta begins reading the Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric and the Glow/Grow/Go feedback protocol, recognising that her instinct to give long, direct corrective feedback after every observation is actually less effective than giving brief, specific, low-inference notes within 24 hours of each visit. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 6 (instructional capacity building) and Permendikbud 6/2018 supervisi duty. At this stage, the leader is not yet running consistent walkthrough cycles, but is becoming aware that observation is a coaching tool, not an inspection tool — and that the language of feedback matters as much as its content.
keyTakeaways: Walkthrough observation (formative) and formal supervision (summative) are distinct practices — both are needed.
Use the 'I noticed / I wonder' format — feedback should be specific, low-inference, and growth-oriented.
Visit every teacher at least once every 14 days — coverage rhythm matters more than visit length.
selfAssessment: I can explain the difference between walkthrough observation and formal supervision.
I have read the Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric and Glow/Grow/Go feedback protocol.
I write feedback in 'I noticed / I wonder' format, not direct prescription.
I deliver walkthrough feedback within 24 hours of the visit.
I can describe what makes feedback developmental versus evaluative.

activity.task: Conduct three walkthroughs this week using the Eduversal protocol. For each, write feedback in 'I noticed / I wonder' format and deliver within 24 hours. Reflect on the differences in teacher response between this and your previous feedback style.
activity.output: Three walkthrough feedback notes + a one-paragraph reflection.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

cial-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand assessment moderation systems and consistency leadership as essential for grading integrity across departments. A leader at this stage can recognise that without structured moderation, two teachers marking the same essay can give grades two boundaries apart — and that this divergence undermines parent trust, university references, and Cambridge Centre standing. For example, a Foundation Representative reviewing Year 11 mock results notices that the Mathematics department's grade distribution diverges 18% from external Cambridge benchmarks; she begins reading about blind-marking + moderation protocols and recognises that her school's moderation has been informal rather than structured. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 7 (assessment leadership) and PP 19/2005 jo. 13/2015 Standar Penilaian. At this stage, the leader is not yet leading moderation cycles, but is becoming aware that grading integrity requires structural protection, not just professional trust.
keyTakeaways: Grading integrity requires structural moderation — blind-marking, calibration, third-marker for divergences.
Compare internal grade distributions against external Cambridge benchmarks regularly.
Moderation protects students, parents, the school's reputation, and the teachers themselves.
selfAssessment: I can explain why moderation matters for grading integrity.
I know my school's current moderation practices.
I can compare internal grade distributions against Cambridge benchmarks.
I have read at least one document on structured moderation protocols.
I can describe what blind-marking is and why it matters.

activity.task: Compare your school's most recent internal Year 11 grade distribution against Cambridge IGCSE benchmarks for the same subject and cohort year. Identify any subject where internal grades diverge by 10% or more. Discuss findings with the relevant Subject Leader.
activity.output: A comparison report + Subject Leader discussion notes.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

cial-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, school leaders run structured assessment moderation cycles termly and use grade-distribution analysis to drive consistency. A leader at this stage establishes blind-marking protocols, runs calibration sessions, follows up divergences with third markers, and tracks year-on-year improvement in inter-rater reliability. For example, an Academic Coordinator in Bandung runs end-of-Sem-1 moderation across all six departments: each department samples 10% of summative work, marks blind, identifies divergences over two grade boundaries, refers them to a third marker, and documents resolutions. Year-on-year analysis shows that internal-vs-Cambridge grade variance has narrowed from 18% to 11% over two years. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 7 + PP 19/2005 Standar Penilaian. At this stage, the leader runs moderation competently within her school but does not yet co-design network-level moderation methodology or coach other Coordinators.
keyTakeaways: Run structured moderation cycles termly: blind-marking, calibration, divergence resolution.
Track year-on-year improvement in inter-rater reliability — this is your evidence of structural integrity.
Document resolutions transparently — moderation outputs become teacher PD inputs.
selfAssessment: I run structured moderation cycles every term.
Blind-marking is the default protocol in my school for summative assessment.
I track inter-rater reliability year-on-year.
Divergences over two grade boundaries are systematically referred to third markers.
I can describe a specific moderation insight that led to teacher PD this year.

activity.task: Run a structured moderation cycle for one summative assessment cycle in your school. Document the protocol, divergences identified, third-marker resolutions, and the teacher-PD output that emerged from the cycle.
activity.output: A moderation cycle report including findings + PD output.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL and discuss with the Principal.

pdpc-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand strategic CPD planning — designing professional development that aligns to school priorities and Cambridge PDQ pathways, rather than collecting whatever PD is convenient or popular. A leader at this stage can recognise that PD allocated by demand is not the same as PD allocated by school improvement evidence, and that Cambridge offers Professional Development Qualifications (PDQ) which provide structured pathways for teacher growth. For example, a newly appointed Academic Coordinator in Surabaya reviews the school's PD spending from the past two years and notices that 70% went to single-day workshops chosen by individual teacher preference, not aligned to the school's improvement priorities; she also discovers Cambridge PDQ pathways and recognises they offer more sustained growth than ad-hoc workshops. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 6.1 (capacity building) and Permendikdasmen 7/2025 on principal-led PD. At this stage, the leader is not yet redesigning PD strategy, but is becoming aware that strategic CPD requires alignment to evidence, not just demand.
keyTakeaways: PD spending should align to school improvement evidence, not just teacher preference.
Cambridge PDQ pathways offer structured, sustained development — investigate whether your teachers are using them.
Audit your last 12 months' PD spending against your School Improvement Plan priorities.
selfAssessment: I can describe my school's PD priorities for this year and the evidence behind them.
I know whether teachers in my school are using Cambridge PDQ pathways.
I can audit PD spending against School Improvement Plan priorities.
I distinguish between demand-driven and evidence-driven PD allocation.
I have discussed strategic CPD design with at least one HQ specialist or external mentor.

activity.task: Audit your school's PD spending from the past 12 months. Map each PD activity to a specific School Improvement Plan priority or Domain Heatmap finding. Identify three PD investments that look ad-hoc and propose realignment to evidence-driven priorities.
activity.output: PD spending audit + three realignment proposals.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL and discuss with the Principal.

pdpc-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand coaching, mentoring, and teacher leadership as the engines of sustained professional learning — not optional extras. A leader at this stage can recognise that workshops alone rarely produce sustained practice change; what makes change stick is the embedded, ongoing coaching relationships that follow. For example, a Principal in Yogyakarta sends three teachers to an external workshop on assessment for learning; she follows up six weeks later and finds that two have not implemented anything from the workshop. She begins reading about coaching cycles and recognises that the missing piece was structured follow-up coaching, not the workshop quality itself. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 6.2 (coaching/mentoring) + Eduversal Mentor-Base certification. At this stage, the leader is not yet running coaching cycles, but is becoming aware that coaching is the missing link between PD attendance and practice change.
keyTakeaways: Workshops alone rarely produce sustained change — coaching follow-up is what makes practice stick.
Identify teachers in your school who could become coaching partners for less-experienced colleagues.
Read at least one coaching cycle document (e.g., GROW model, Cognitive Coaching framework).
selfAssessment: I can explain why coaching follow-up matters more than workshop quality alone.
I have identified at least three potential coach/mentor pairs in my school.
I have read one coaching framework document this year.
I can describe what a coaching cycle structure looks like.
I have engaged in coaching myself, either as coach or as coachee, in the past 12 months.

activity.task: Identify three potential coach-mentee pairings in your school based on Domain Heatmap data: pair a teacher strong in Domain X with a teacher developing in Domain X. Propose a six-week coaching cycle for one pair. Discuss with the Principal.
activity.output: Three potential pairings + one detailed coaching cycle proposal.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

pdpc-3_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, school leaders run structured coaching and mentoring cycles consistently, build coaching capacity in others, and use coaching as the primary mechanism for embedding professional learning. A leader at this stage establishes coach-mentee pairings based on evidence (Domain Heatmaps, IPDL goals), runs structured cycles using a clear framework (GROW, Cognitive Coaching), monitors implementation, and develops other teachers as coaches. For example, an Academic Coordinator in Bandung establishes coaching trios across her school: each trio meets fortnightly for six weeks, with one teacher in the coachee role each week and the other two as critical friends. She trains the team using the GROW framework, observes their cycles, and gradually develops three teacher-leaders who can run their own coaching trios next year. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 6.2 + Eduversal teacher-leader development pathway. At this stage, the leader runs coaching competently within her school but does not yet lead network-level coaching capacity-building or coach other Coordinators.
keyTakeaways: Establish structured coaching cycles using an explicit framework — not informal mentoring.
Build coaching capacity in others — your goal is to make yourself less essential, not more.
Use Domain Heatmap evidence to design coach-coachee pairings, not just personal preference.
selfAssessment: I run at least three structured coaching cycles per year.
Coaching pairings are based on Domain Heatmap or IPDL evidence.
I have trained at least two other teachers in coaching frameworks this year.
I monitor coaching cycle implementation, not just initiate them.
I can describe a teacher who became a coach because of capacity I built.

activity.task: Establish a coaching trio in your school. Train the team in a coaching framework. Run one full six-week cycle. Observe at least one session. Document what worked, what to adjust, and what the trio's next cycle will focus on.
activity.output: Coaching trio cycle log + facilitator reflection.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

pdpc-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand professional learning culture — the shared norms, habits, and rituals that make a school a learning organisation rather than just a delivery operation. A leader at this stage can recognise that a school where teachers genuinely engage in PLCs, share student work, observe each other, and reflect together is fundamentally different from a school where compliance is performed and real learning happens privately. For example, a newly appointed Principal in Solo notices that her PLCs technically run on schedule but conversation stays at the logistics level — no one shares struggling lessons, no one brings student work for collective examination, no one questions practice. She begins reading about professional learning communities (DuFour, Lipton & Wellman) and recognises that the form is in place but the culture is not. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 6.1 + Eduversal PLC governance. At this stage, the leader is not yet redesigning the culture, but is becoming aware that PLC compliance and PLC culture are not the same thing.
keyTakeaways: PLC compliance (the meeting happens) and PLC culture (real learning happens) are not the same thing.
Read at least one professional learning community text (DuFour, Lipton & Wellman, Hargreaves).
Notice the gap between scheduled PLC time and substantive professional learning conversation in your school.
selfAssessment: I can describe what a professional learning culture looks like in practice.
I have read at least one PLC text this academic year.
I can identify whether my school's PLCs perform compliance or culture.
I notice when PLC conversation stays at logistics rather than instructional dialogue.
I can describe one ritual or habit that signals genuine learning culture.

activity.task: Attend three PLCs in your school as observer. Document what proportion of conversation focused on logistics vs instructional dialogue, and what proportion of teachers shared substantive practice (struggling lessons, student work, real questions). Reflect on what that reveals about the culture.
activity.output: PLC observation log + culture reflection.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL and discuss with the Principal.

pdpc-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, school leaders actively cultivate professional learning culture through specific structures, language, and rituals — not just through scheduled PLC time. A leader at this stage models vulnerability (sharing her own struggling practice), normalises reflection on failed lessons, builds explicit norms about how teachers talk about each other and about students, and protects time for substantive professional dialogue. For example, an Academic Coordinator in Tangerang opens every monthly Strategy PLC by sharing one of her own teaching observations from the month — including one she felt she handled poorly. This modelling shifts what teachers feel willing to share, and over time the PLC moves from logistics-dominated to substantive. She also establishes a 'Bright Spot' opening ritual where each teacher shares one observed strength they saw in a colleague's lesson that month. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 6.1 + Eduversal Domain 6 collaboration. At this stage, the leader cultivates culture competently within her school but does not yet share methodology with the broader Eduversal network or coach other Coordinators on culture-building.
keyTakeaways: Model vulnerability about your own practice — culture follows behaviour, not exhortation.
Establish explicit rituals (Bright Spot openings, struggling-lesson shares) that signal what's valued.
Protect substantive professional dialogue time — when logistics intrudes, redirect explicitly.
selfAssessment: I model vulnerability about my own teaching/leadership in PLC settings.
My PLCs have explicit norms about how we talk about teachers and students.
Substantive professional dialogue is the dominant mode in my PLCs.
I have established at least one ritual that signals what professional learning means in our school.
I can describe how PLC culture has changed in my school across the past year.

activity.task: Design and lead one PLC that explicitly cultivates professional learning culture — through your own modelling, an explicit norm, and a ritual. Document the design, run the PLC, and reflect on what shifted.
activity.output: PLC design + facilitation reflection.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL and discuss with the Principal.

ewsc-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand school climate, values, and community identity as deliberate constructions — not accidents of personality or tradition. A leader at this stage can recognise that a school's climate is not 'just how it is' — it is shaped by leadership behaviour, language, rituals, and how disagreements get handled in public. For example, a newly promoted Foundation Representative in Yogyakarta reflects on what makes her school feel different from other schools she has visited; she notices that what she initially attributed to 'personality' (the warm welcome at reception, the way teachers correct students publicly vs privately, the rituals around morning assembly) is actually a result of accumulated leadership choices. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 5 (school culture) + Permendiknas 13/2007 kompetensi sosial. At this stage, the leader is not yet shaping climate intentionally, but is becoming aware that climate is shaped, not inherited.
keyTakeaways: School climate is shaped by leadership behaviour and language — it is not inherited or accidental.
Notice what your school does at the small level (welcome, correction, ritual) — that is climate in action.
Read at least one text on school culture (Deal & Peterson, Schein, or Indonesian-context equivalents).
selfAssessment: I can describe my school's climate beyond 'good' or 'positive' — using specific behaviours.
I can identify three small behaviours in my school that reveal something about its values.
I have read at least one school culture text this year.
I can describe how climate has shifted in my school across leadership transitions.
I notice when my own leadership behaviour shapes climate moments.

activity.task: Walk through your school for one full day as observer. Document three small moments (welcome, correction, ritual, transition) that reveal climate in action. Reflect on what each communicates about the school's values.
activity.output: Three-moment climate observation + values interpretation.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

eao-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand ethical decision-making and accountability as core leadership disciplines — not occasional dilemmas. A leader at this stage can recognise that everyday decisions (teacher promotion, parent grievance, student discipline, procurement) all carry ethical weight, and that transparent reasoning + accountability behaviour build or erode school trust over time. For example, a newly appointed Academic Coordinator in Bandung reflects on a recent decision to allow one teacher special schedule accommodation while declining a similar request from another; she initially defends the decision as 'context-dependent' but begins to recognise that without transparent reasoning, contextual decisions can look like favouritism. She begins reading about ethical leadership frameworks. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 4 (ethics) + Permendiknas 13/2007 kompetensi kepribadian. At this stage, the leader is not yet using a structured ethical decision-making framework, but is becoming aware that consistent, transparent reasoning is itself an ethical practice.
keyTakeaways: Everyday decisions carry ethical weight — promotion, grievance handling, procurement, discipline.
Consistent + transparent reasoning is itself an ethical practice — not just intent.
Read at least one ethical leadership text or framework (Starratt, Sergiovanni, or local equivalents).
selfAssessment: I can identify three recent decisions I made that carried ethical weight.
I can describe my reasoning for each transparently.
I have read at least one ethical leadership text this year.
I notice when ethical considerations are absent from a decision conversation.
I have explained my reasoning for a decision to a stakeholder who initially disagreed.

activity.task: Document three recent leadership decisions you made. For each, write out (a) the context, (b) your reasoning, (c) the principle that guided you, (d) what you would tell someone who disagreed. Identify which decisions are easy to articulate and which are not.
activity.output: Three-decision ethical reflection log.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

eao-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand operational management and resource stewardship as strategic leadership functions — not delegated administrative tasks. A leader at this stage can recognise that how budget is allocated, how facilities are stewarded, and how resources are deployed all communicate priorities more loudly than any policy document. For example, a newly promoted School Principal in Surabaya inherits a school where 60% of the technology budget goes to single-classroom hardware that few teachers use, while a documented teacher-PD priority for the year remains unfunded. She recognises that the budget reveals what is actually prioritised, regardless of what the SIP says. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 9 (operations) + Permendikbud 6/2018 managerial duty + 8 SNP Standar Pembiayaan. At this stage, the leader is not yet redesigning resource allocation strategy, but is becoming aware that operational decisions are strategic communication — not administrative bookkeeping.
keyTakeaways: Operational decisions are strategic communication — what gets funded reveals priorities.
Audit the gap between stated priorities (SIP, RKAS) and actual spending allocation.
Read at least one text on resource stewardship as leadership practice.
selfAssessment: I can describe my school's spending allocation across major categories.
I have audited the gap between stated SIP priorities and actual spending this year.
I can identify three resource-allocation decisions that reveal what the school actually prioritises.
I notice when operational decisions get treated as administrative rather than strategic.
I have read at least one resource stewardship text this year.

activity.task: Audit your school's RKAS spending from the most recent quarter against the School Improvement Plan priorities. Identify three specific gaps where spending does not align with stated priorities. Discuss findings with the Principal or yayasan representative.
activity.output: RKAS-vs-SIP gap audit + discussion notes.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

fcep-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand community partnerships and external stakeholder engagement as strategic resources — not optional outreach. A leader at this stage can recognise that local universities, employers, alumni, professional associations, and community organisations are potential partners who can extend student opportunities (internships, mentorship, scholarships, real-world projects) far beyond what the school can do alone. For example, a Foundation Representative in Bandung reflects that her school has graduated talented students for 15 years but has no structured alumni engagement, no university partnerships, no employer-mentorship programme — opportunities her students do not have because the school has not built the relationships. She begins mapping potential community partnership opportunities. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 3 (community engagement) + Permendiknas 13/2007 kompetensi sosial. At this stage, the leader is not yet building partnerships systematically, but is becoming aware that community partnerships extend educational opportunity in ways the school alone cannot.
keyTakeaways: Community partnerships are strategic resources for extending student opportunity.
Map potential partners: universities, alumni, employers, professional associations, community organisations.
Begin one structured partnership conversation per term — networking is leadership work, not extra work.
selfAssessment: I can name three current external partners my school engages with meaningfully.
I can identify five potential partners we have not yet engaged.
I have had at least one structured partnership conversation this term.
I see partnership-building as leadership work, not extra work.
I can describe one student opportunity that exists because of an external partnership.

activity.task: Map five potential community partnership opportunities your school has not yet engaged: identify the partner, the student opportunity it could create, and the first specific conversation you would initiate to explore it. Discuss with the Principal.
activity.output: Five-partnership opportunity map + initiation plan.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

fcep-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand the Cambridge professional network as accessible — not reserved for senior leadership or annual conferences. A leader at this stage can recognise that Cambridge offers regional events, online communities, leader forums, and direct contact with Cambridge regional representatives, and that engagement with these brings external benchmarking + peer learning + visibility for the school. For example, a newly appointed School Principal in Jakarta discovers that Cambridge holds a regional school leadership event in Singapore each year that she could attend; she also discovers a Cambridge online forum for school leaders where principals from across South-East Asia discuss centre-level challenges. She had assumed these were only for very senior or veteran principals. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards 6.1 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the leader is not yet representing the school in Cambridge networks, but is becoming aware that the network is accessible and that engagement is professional development, not just networking.
keyTakeaways: Cambridge professional networks are accessible to any partner-school leader, not just senior or veteran principals.
Regional events, online forums, and direct regional-rep contact all offer external benchmarking + peer learning.
Begin one Cambridge network engagement per term — even an online forum participation counts.
selfAssessment: I know how to access Cambridge's school leader resources and forums.
I have engaged with at least one Cambridge online community this academic year.
I can name three Cambridge regional events relevant to my role.
I have had contact with my Cambridge regional representative.
I see Cambridge network engagement as professional development.

activity.task: Register for the Cambridge Schools Online leader resources. Identify three engagement opportunities: one regional event, one online forum, and one direct contact (regional rep, peer principal in Indonesia). Initiate one engagement this term.
activity.output: Engagement registration record + first-engagement reflection.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

fcep-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, school leaders are beginning to understand university articulation and pathway partnerships as deliberate, long-term commitments — not just college fairs and recommendation letters. A leader at this stage can recognise that strong university pathway outcomes for students (admissions, scholarships, programme fit) depend on multi-year relationships between the school and target universities, on understanding what universities actually look for, and on coaching students to build authentic profiles over time. For example, a Foundation Representative in Bandung observes that her school's graduating cohort has strong Cambridge IGCSE and AS-A Level results but the university destinations are concentrated in a narrow band of local universities — students with the academic profile to apply globally are not doing so because no one is helping them build that pathway. She begins reading about university articulation strategy. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 3 + Permendiknas 13/2007 kompetensi sosial. At this stage, the leader is not yet building structured articulation partnerships, but is becoming aware that university outcomes are products of multi-year strategy, not last-minute application support.
keyTakeaways: University outcomes depend on multi-year articulation strategy, not last-minute application support.
Map your school's actual university destinations across recent cohorts — the pattern reveals where your strategy is concentrated and where opportunities are missed.
Begin one structured university articulation conversation per term — with universities, with current student cohorts, with families.
selfAssessment: I can describe my school's university destination pattern across recent cohorts.
I can identify universities aligned with my students' academic profile that they currently underapply to.
I have had at least one structured conversation with a university about articulation this academic year.
I see university outcomes as products of multi-year strategy.
I can describe one student pathway that was strengthened by school-university partnership work.

activity.task: Audit your school's university destinations across the last three graduating cohorts. Identify three universities where your students have the academic profile to apply but do not. Initiate one structured articulation conversation with one of those universities or with a current cohort about that pathway.
activity.output: University destination audit + one articulation initiation record.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.

fcep-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, school leaders maintain structured university articulation partnerships across multiple destination universities, coordinate counselling cycles that produce strong fit-based applications, and track outcomes year-over-year. A leader at this stage works with at least three university partners on structured articulation (campus visits, admissions Q&A, joint information evenings), runs a multi-year university counselling cycle that begins in Year 9, and uses outcome data to refine the school's strategy. For example, an Academic Coordinator in Tangerang has built articulation relationships with two Singapore universities and one UK university over three years; her school now sends 5-7 students per cohort to international destinations they previously did not apply to, and her counselling cycle starts in Year 9 with academic mapping rather than Year 12 with application crisis. This practice aligns with Cambridge School Leader Standards area 3 + Eduversal Network outcome ambition. At this stage, the leader runs articulation competently within her school but does not yet contribute network-level methodology or coach other Coordinators on pathway strategy.
keyTakeaways: Build structured articulation partnerships with at least three universities — visits, joint events, admissions contact.
Run multi-year counselling cycles starting in Year 9 — university decisions are made in Year 9, not Year 12.
Track outcomes year-over-year and use the data to refine strategy.
selfAssessment: I have at least three structured university articulation partnerships.
Counselling cycles in my school start in Year 9, not Year 12.
I track university outcomes year-over-year and use the data to refine strategy.
I can describe specific student outcomes that exist because of articulation strategy.
I have shared articulation methodology with at least one peer school.

activity.task: Document your school's articulation partnership portfolio. Map each partnership against student outcomes over the past three years. Identify one partnership to deepen and one new partnership to initiate based on cohort needs. Present to the Principal or yayasan representative.
activity.output: Articulation portfolio document + deepen/initiate plan + presentation materials.
activity.evidence: Upload to your IPDL.
